questions.q: What does CD usually stand for in delivery pipelines?
questions.a: Continuous Delivery or Continuous Deployment, depending on context.
A versioned output such as a binary, image, or package produced by the pipeline.
A workflow where most work lands on a shared main branch in small increments.
Running two production environments and switching traffic between them.
Releasing a change to a small slice of traffic before full rollout.

questions.q: Why do build caches matter in CI?
questions.a: They reduce rebuild time by reusing unchanged dependencies or layers.
They reduce confidence in pipeline results and hide real regressions.
The exact same artifact is promoted between environments, reducing drift.

questions.q: Why do feature flags help delivery?
questions.a: They decouple deployment from release so you can control exposure safely.
Git becomes the source of truth for desired state and change review.

questions.q: Why is rollback planning part of the pipeline design?
questions.a: A fast rollback reduces blast radius when a release misbehaves.

questions.q: When would you prefer canary over blue-green?
questions.a: When you want progressive risk reduction and production signal before full rollout.

questions.q: When is a monorepo pipeline harder to operate?
questions.a: When builds become too coupled and every change triggers too much work.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with very strict gates?
questions.a: They catch more issues, but they can also slow delivery and frustrate teams.

questions.q: Why do teams promote the same artifact rather than rebuild per environment?
questions.a: It ensures dev, staging, and prod all run the same tested bits.

questions.q: What is the downside of overusing feature flags?
questions.a: Flag debt builds up and creates hidden branches of behavior.

questions.q: When should you treat infrastructure as code as the source of truth?
questions.a: When reproducibility and reviewability matter more than ad hoc clicks.
